text.title: Statistical Anti-patterns in Growth Experiments
text.summary: Six common mistakes that invalidate A/B test results and how to avoid them.

antipattern.description: Peeking at results and stopping early when the desired direction appears.
antipattern.reason: Each early look inflates the cumulative false positive rate above alpha. A test peeked 5 times at p=0.05 has a true false positive rate near 19%.

antipattern.description: Using one-tailed tests by default to get lower p-values.
antipattern.reason: One-tailed tests assume you would never ship the variant if it regressed. This is rarely true and halves the threshold, doubling false positives in the expected direction.

antipattern.description: Stopping the test as soon as p < 0.05, regardless of pre-registered sample size.
antipattern.reason: P-values fluctuate during data collection. The test is only valid at the pre-registered sample size or end date — not at any earlier random crossing of alpha.

antipattern.description: Testing 10 metrics and reporting the one that reached significance.
antipattern.reason: Multiple comparisons: with 10 independent tests at alpha=0.05, expected false positives = 0.5. Apply Bonferroni correction (alpha/k) or designate one primary metric before launch.

antipattern.description: Treating "not significant" as proof that there is no effect.
antipattern.reason: Absence of evidence is not evidence of absence. A non-significant result in an underpowered test is uninformative. Say "insufficient evidence" not "no effect".

antipattern.description: Ignoring practical significance when statistical significance is achieved.
antipattern.reason: A +0.01% lift can be statistically significant at N=10M but economically irrelevant. Always check whether the CI lower bound justifies shipping cost and ongoing maintenance.
